question:
3. Prove that if the family of integral curves of the differential equation
\[
\frac{d y}{d x}+p(x) y=q(x) \quad p(x) \cdot q(x) \neq 0
\]
is cut by the line \( x=k \), the tangents at the points of intersection are concurrent.
solution:
Solution. The equation of the line tangent to the smooth curve \( y=f(x) \) at the point \( (k, m) \) is
\[
y-m=f^{\prime}(k)(x-k)
\]

If \( f \) is a solution of the given differential equation this becomes
\[
y-m=[q(k)-m p(k)](x-k)
\]

For any value of \( m \), this line passes through the point
\[
\left(k+\frac{1}{p(k)}, \frac{q(k)}{p(k)}\right)
\]

descriptive_long.question:
3. Prove that if the family of integral curves of the differential equation
\[
\frac{d\, ordinate}{d\, abscissa}+coefone(abscissa)\, ordinate = coeftwo(abscissa) \quad coefone(abscissa) \cdot coeftwo(abscissa) \neq 0
\]
is cut by the line \( abscissa = cutline \), the tangents at the points of intersection are concurrent.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The equation of the line tangent to the smooth curve \( ordinate = curvefn(abscissa) \) at the point \( (cutline, valueat) \) is
\[
ordinate - valueat = curvefn^{\prime}(cutline)(abscissa - cutline)
\]

If \( curvefn \) is a solution of the given differential equation this becomes
\[
ordinate - valueat = [ coeftwo(cutline) - valueat\, coefone(cutline) ] ( abscissa - cutline )
\]

For any value of \( valueat \), this line passes through the point
\[
\left( cutline + \frac{1}{ coefone(cutline) }, \frac{ coeftwo(cutline) }{ coefone(cutline) } \right)
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
3. Prove that if the family of integral curves of the differential equation
\[
\frac{d toothbrush}{d paperclip}+lumberjack(paperclip) toothbrush=marzipans(paperclip) \quad lumberjack(paperclip) \cdot marzipans(paperclip) \neq 0
\]
is cut by the line \( paperclip=horseshoe \), the tangents at the points of intersection are concurrent.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The equation of the line tangent to the smooth curve \( toothbrush=caterpillar(paperclip) \) at the point \( (horseshoe, chandelier) \) is
\[
toothbrush-chandelier=caterpillar^{\prime}(horseshoe)(paperclip-horseshoe)
\]

If \( caterpillar \) is a solution of the given differential equation this becomes
\[
toothbrush-chandelier=[marzipans(horseshoe)-chandelier lumberjack(horseshoe)](paperclip-horseshoe)
\]

For any value of \( chandelier \), this line passes through the point
\[
\left(horseshoe+\frac{1}{lumberjack(horseshoe)}, \frac{marzipans(horseshoe)}{lumberjack(horseshoe)}\right)
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
3. Prove that if the family of integral curves of the differential equation
\[
\frac{d horizontalaxis}{d verticalaxis}+steadystate(verticalaxis) horizontalaxis=zeroquantity(verticalaxis) \quad steadystate(verticalaxis) \cdot zeroquantity(verticalaxis) \neq 0
\]
is cut by the line \( verticalaxis=variablepoint \), the tangents at the points of intersection are concurrent.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The equation of the line tangent to the smooth curve \( horizontalaxis=constantvalue(verticalaxis) \) at the point \( (variablepoint, dynamicdip) \) is
\[
horizontalaxis-dynamicdip=constantvalue^{\prime}(variablepoint)(verticalaxis-variablepoint)
\]

If constantvalue is a solution of the given differential equation this becomes
\[
horizontalaxis-dynamicdip=[zeroquantity(variablepoint)-dynamicdip\; steadystate(variablepoint)](verticalaxis-variablepoint)
\]

For any value of dynamicdip, this line passes through the point
\[
\left(variablepoint+\frac{1}{steadystate(variablepoint)}, \frac{zeroquantity(variablepoint)}{steadystate(variablepoint)}\right)
\]

garbled_string.question:
3. Prove that if the family of integral curves of the differential equation
\[
\frac{d wfcznuro}{d tqabvzye}+qrsdplak(tqabvzye) wfcznuro=zmytcoha(tqabvzye) \quad qrsdplak(tqabvzye) \cdot zmytcoha(tqabvzye) \neq 0
\]
is cut by the line \( tqabvzye=jvwdurto \), the tangents at the points of intersection are concurrent.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The equation of the line tangent to the smooth curve \( wfcznuro=glsproqe(tqabvzye) \) at the point \( (jvwdurto, xhvyreot) \) is
\[
wfcznuro-xhvyreot=glsproqe^{\prime}(jvwdurto)(tqabvzye-jvwdurto)
\]

If \( glsproqe \) is a solution of the given differential equation this becomes
\[
wfcznuro-xhvyreot=[zmytcoha(jvwdurto)-xhvyreot qrsdplak(jvwdurto)](tqabvzye-jvwdurto)
\]

For any value of \( xhvyreot \), this line passes through the point
\[
\left(jvwdurto+\frac{1}{qrsdplak(jvwdurto)}, \frac{zmytcoha(jvwdurto)}{qrsdplak(jvwdurto)}\right)
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $I\subset\mathbb R$ be an open interval containing the fixed point $a$.  
For an integer $n\ge 1$ let  

  $A:I\longrightarrow M_{n}(\mathbb R),\qquad b:I\longrightarrow\mathbb R^{n}$  

be $C^{1}$-maps, and consider the inhomogeneous linear system  

(1)  $Y'(x)=A(x)\,Y(x)+b(x)\qquad (x\in I),\qquad Y:I\to\mathbb R^{n}\;.$  

Every solution $Y$ defines a space-curve  
  $\Gamma_{Y}:x\longmapsto\bigl(x,Y(x)\bigr)\subset\mathbb R^{\,n+1}$.  
Denote by $\ell_{Y}$ the tangent line to $\Gamma_{Y}$ at the point  
$P_{Y}:=(a,Y(a))$.

(a)  Show that the family $\{\ell_{Y}\}_{Y\text{ solves (1)}}$ of lines is a pencil
     (i.e. all the lines pass through one common point) iff the constant matrix
     $A(a)$ is a non-zero scalar multiple of the identity:
             $\displaystyle A(a)=p\,I_{n}\;(p\ne0).$

(b)  If the equivalent condition in (a) holds, determine the common intersection
     point $P^{\!*}$ of all the lines $\ell_{Y}$ explicitly in terms of $p$ and
     $b(a)$.

(c)  Give a concrete $2\times2$ example in which $A(a)$ is not a scalar matrix
     and verify directly (without appealing to (a)) that the corresponding
     tangent lines are **not** concurrent.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
  $A_{0}:=A(a)\in M_{n}(\mathbb R),\qquad b_{0}:=b(a)\in\mathbb R^{n}.$  

Step 1.  An equation for $\ell_{Y}$.  
For an arbitrary solution $Y$ put $Y_{0}:=Y(a)$.  From (1) we have  
  $Y'(a)=A_{0}Y_{0}+b_{0}=:V_{0}.$  
Hence the tangent line at $P_{Y}=(a,Y_{0})$ is  

(2)  $\ell_{Y}(t):=(a,Y_{0})+t\,(1,V_{0})\qquad(t\in\mathbb R).$

Step 2.  A necessary algebraic condition for concurrence.  
Assume there exists a point  
  $P^{\!*}=(x^{\!*},y^{\!*})\in\mathbb R^{\,n+1}$  
such that $P^{\!*}\in\ell_{Y}$ for **every** solution $Y$.  
For a particular solution the parameter value $t$ needed in (2) is forced by
the $x$-coordinate:

(3)  $t=x^{\!*}-a=:t^{\!*}\quad\text{(same $t^{\!*}$ for all $Y$).}$  

Using (2)-(3) the $y$-coordinates give  

(4)  $y^{\!*}=Y_{0}+t^{\!*}\,(A_{0}Y_{0}+b_{0})
         =\bigl(I_{n}+t^{\!*}A_{0}\bigr)Y_{0}+t^{\!*}b_{0}\quad
           \text{for \emph{every}}\,Y_{0}\in\mathbb R^{n}.$

Independence of $Y_{0}$ forces  

(5)  $I_{n}+t^{\!*}A_{0}=0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
         A_{0}=-\frac1{t^{\!*}}\,I_{n}=:p\,I_{n},\;p\ne0.$

Thus $A(a)$ must be a non-zero scalar matrix.  This proves the ``only if'' part
of (a).

Step 3.  Sufficiency.  
Conversely suppose $A_{0}=p\,I_{n}$ with $p\ne0$.  Choose  

(6)  $t^{\!*}:=-\dfrac1{p},\qquad 
         P^{\!*}:=\bigl(a+t^{\!*},\,y^{\!*}\bigr),\;
         y^{\!*}:=-\dfrac{b_{0}}{p}.$

For an arbitrary solution $Y$ formula (2) with $t=t^{\!*}$ yields  

$(a+t^{\!*},\,Y_{0}+t^{\!*}(pY_{0}+b_{0}))
=(a+t^{\!*},\,-\tfrac{b_{0}}{p})=P^{\!*},$

so $P^{\!*}\in\ell_{Y}$.  Hence all lines are concurrent at $P^{\!*}$, proving
the ``if'' part and completing (a)-(b).

Step 4.  A non-concurrent example (part (c)).  
Let $n=2$, choose  

$A(x)=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}$ (so $A(a)=A_{0}$ is \emph{not}
a scalar matrix) and $b(x)\equiv(0,0)^{T}$.  
For initial data $Y_{0}=(y_{1},y_{2})^{T}$ one solves (1) near $x=a$:

$Y'(a)=A_{0}Y_{0}=(y_{1}+y_{2},\,2y_{2})^{T}.$  

The tangent line (2) becomes  

$(x,y_{1},y_{2})=(a,y_{1},y_{2})+t\,(1,y_{1}+y_{2},2y_{2}).$

Taking two particular starting points, say $Y_{0}^{(1)}=(1,0)^{T}$ and
$Y_{0}^{(2)}=(0,1)^{T}$, one obtains the distinct tangent lines  

$\ell_{1}:\;(x,y_{1},y_{2})=(a,1,0)+t\,(1,1,0),$  

$\ell_{2}:\;(x,y_{1},y_{2})=(a,0,1)+t\,(1,1,2).$  

Solving the linear system for a putative intersection point shows that
no common solution $(x,y_{1},y_{2})$ exists, so the lines are not concurrent,
as predicted by part (a).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• The original problem involves a single first-order scalar ODE and planar
  geometry; a single differentiation and a one–line substitution give the
  answer.  
• The enhanced variant replaces the scalar ODE by a \emph{vector} inhomogeneous
  system, embeds the curves in the higher-dimensional space
  $\mathbb R^{\,n+1}$, and asks for a complete characterisation
  (necessary \emph{and} sufficient conditions) of when concurrency occurs.  
  This forces the solver to  
  – write the tangent lines in vector form,  
  – analyse the resulting condition (4) for \emph{all} initial vectors
    $Y_{0}$,  
  – recognise and solve the matrix equation
    $I_{n}+tA_{0}=0$,  
  – connect the algebraic outcome to geometric concurrency, and  
  – produce a counter-example when the condition fails.  
• These steps require linear algebra over matrices (invertibility,
  identification of scalar matrices), elementary but non-trivial projective
  geometry in $\mathbb R^{\,n+1}$, and explicit construction of solutions to
  verify the negative case.  
• Even for $n=2$ the general condition is not obvious; for arbitrary $n$ the
  solver must argue abstractly rather than by direct coordinates.
  Hence the problem is substantially more sophisticated than the original and
  cannot be dispatched by simple pattern matching.
